topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in my apartment was deafening, a stark contrast to the vibrant chatter I’d been anticipating. I kept glancing at the calendar, a frantic energy thrumming beneath my skin. Every notification, every casual text from the group, felt like a cruel tease. When the final email arrived—a terse notification stating the gathering was off—the air seemed to evacuate my lungs. My hands, which had been fidgeting with a loose thread on my sweater, went still. A coldness, sharp and immediate, settled in my chest, a physical clench that made my breath catch. I stared at the screen, the bright light reflecting off my wide eyes, a strange, hollow ache blooming where excitement had been. I didn't move, just sat there, the phantom warmth of shared celebration suddenly replaced by an unsettling, empty chill.

[story 2]

"No, seriously?" The words sounded thin, reedy, swallowed by the quiet of the living room. I replayed the message over and over, the casual dismissal in the sender's tone grating on my nerves. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I pushed myself up from the sofa, needing movement, needing to do *something* other than sit paralyzed by this sudden deflation. As I walked to the window, I watched the streetlights flicker on, each one a tiny, indifferent star. A hot flush crept up my neck, a sensation entirely unrelated to the cool night air. My fingers traced the condensation on the glass, trying to anchor myself to something solid, anything, because the sudden lack of expected attention left me feeling utterly adrift, a ship without a compass.

[story 3]

I reread the cancellation notice three times, the words blurring slightly at the edges. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a profound sense of being overlooked, a sudden deflation of the entire world's gravity. A strange, tight pressure built behind my sternum, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I ran a hand through my hair, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. I felt a desperate need to argue, to demand an explanation, but the words caught in my throat, leaving only a dry, rasping sound. I stood by the door, listening to the muffled sounds of the world outside, feeling intensely vulnerable, as if the very ground beneath my feet had shifted, leaving me unsteady and exposed.
